governor.name: Maura Healey
governor.title: Governor
governor.background: Maura Healey is the 73rd Governor of Massachusetts, sworn in January 5, 2023 — the first openly lesbian governor elected in United States history and the second woman to serve as Governor of Massachusetts. She previously served two terms as Massachusetts Attorney General (2015–2023).
governor.backgroundFull:
Maura Healey is the 73rd Governor of Massachusetts, sworn in on January 5, 2023, making history as the first openly lesbian governor elected in the United States and the second woman to serve as Governor of Massachusetts. She serves alongside Lt. Governor Kim Driscoll.

Before her election as governor, Healey served two terms as Massachusetts Attorney General (2015–2023), where she earned a national reputation as a leading consumer advocate and civil rights defender. As AG, she led a 50-state coalition opposing the Trump administration's family separation policy at the border, filed landmark opioid litigation against Purdue Pharma resulting in a $4.9 billion settlement, and brought one of the first state-level climate deception lawsuits against ExxonMobil.

Healey was born in Hampton, New Hampshire, and raised in Grafton, Vermont. She earned her B.A. from Harvard College — where she was a standout player on the women's basketball team — and later played professional basketball in Austria before returning to earn her J.D. from Northeastern University School of Law. She joined the Massachusetts Attorney General's office in 2007 as an Assistant AG in the Civil Rights Division, where she helped lead the successful state challenge to the federal Defense of Marriage Act (DOMA).

As Governor, Healey has prioritized housing affordability, clean energy transition, and economic competitiveness. She signed the Affordable Homes Act in 2024 — a $5.16 billion bond bill described as the largest housing investment in Massachusetts history — and has pushed for accelerated permitting reform and zoning changes to increase housing production. Her administration has also focused on offshore wind energy development, positioning Massachusetts as a national leader in the clean energy transition.

The Governor's office oversees the entire executive branch, including MassDOT (transportation), DESE (education), MassHealth, and economic development programs administered through MassDevelopment and the Executive Office of Economic Development.

sources.label: Office of the Governor — mass.gov
Mass.gov — Governor Healey Official Page
National Governors Association Profile

ltGovernor.name: Kim Driscoll
ltGovernor.title: Lieutenant Governor
ltGovernor.background: Kim Driscoll is the Lieutenant Governor of Massachusetts, serving alongside Governor Maura Healey since January 2023. She previously served as Mayor of Salem, Massachusetts for 15 years (2006–2023), during which she transformed Salem's economy and became one of the most respected municipal executives in New England.
ltGovernor.backgroundFull:
Kim Driscoll is the Lieutenant Governor of Massachusetts, sworn in alongside Governor Maura Healey on January 5, 2023. She is one of the most substantive holders of the office in recent state history, playing an active day-to-day role in the administration rather than serving in a largely ceremonial capacity.

Before becoming Lieutenant Governor, Driscoll served as Mayor of Salem, Massachusetts, from January 2006 through January 2023 — a tenure of nearly 17 years that made her one of the longest-serving mayors in the Commonwealth. Under her leadership, Salem revitalized its downtown economy, expanded the city's internationally known Halloween tourism industry into a year-round economic driver, and produced hundreds of units of new affordable and market-rate housing. She navigated significant fiscal challenges including the 2008 recession and the COVID-19 pandemic while maintaining Salem's bond rating and municipal services.

Driscoll was born and raised in Rhode Island and earned her undergraduate degree from Providence College and her J.D. from Northeastern University School of Law. She worked as an attorney and policy consultant before entering elected office.

As Lieutenant Governor, Driscoll chairs the Governor's Council on Climate Preparedness and Resiliency, oversees workforce development initiatives, and serves as the administration's primary liaison to cities and towns. Her decade and a half of municipal executive experience gives her exceptional credibility with local officials across the Commonwealth.

The Lieutenant Governor's office is particularly relevant for municipal finance, regional planning, and climate resilience initiatives — areas where Driscoll has focused significant attention.

sources.label: Office of the Lt. Governor — mass.gov
Mass.gov — Lt. Governor Driscoll Official Page
Boston Globe — Kim Driscoll wields power as Lt. Governor (Apr 2024)

attorneyGeneral.name: Andrea Campbell
attorneyGeneral.title: Attorney General
attorneyGeneral.background: Andrea Campbell is the 37th Attorney General of Massachusetts, sworn in January 19, 2023 — the first Black woman to serve as Attorney General in state history. A former Boston City Councilor and Council President, she has focused on consumer protection, civil rights enforcement, and environmental justice.
attorneyGeneral.backgroundFull:
Andrea Campbell is the 37th Attorney General of Massachusetts, sworn in on January 19, 2023, making history as the first Black woman to serve as Attorney General in Massachusetts. Her life story is extraordinary: she was born in Los Angeles and raised primarily in foster care and group homes following the incarceration of both her parents. The loss of her twin brother while in state custody deeply shaped her approach to criminal justice and accountability in government.

Campbell graduated from Boston College and earned her J.D. from UCLA School of Law. She returned to Boston and built a career in community advocacy before winning election to the Boston City Council in 2015, representing District 4 (Dorchester, Mattapan, and Jamaica Plain). She served as Council President in 2018–2019, during which she championed police accountability legislation, youth employment, and equitable city budget processes.

As Attorney General, Campbell has pursued an aggressive enforcement agenda in several areas: consumer protection (targeting predatory lending, junk fees, and deceptive marketing practices); environmental justice (prosecuting industrial polluters in overburdened communities); and civil rights (defending abortion access, LGBTQ+ protections, and immigrant rights under Massachusetts law). Her office has also taken a leading role on pharmaceutical industry accountability and fentanyl crisis response.

The Attorney General's office is the primary state resource for filing consumer complaints, reporting wage theft, and accessing civil rights protections. The AG's Consumer Protection Division handles disputes with businesses, landlords, and service providers that cannot be resolved directly.

sources.label: Office of the Attorney General — mass.gov
Mass.gov — Attorney General Campbell Biography

secretaryOfState.name: William Galvin
secretaryOfState.title: Secretary of State
secretaryOfState.background: William Galvin is the Secretary of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts, first elected in 1994 and the longest-serving constitutional officer in state history. His office oversees elections, corporate filings, securities regulation, public records, and the Massachusetts Archives.
secretaryOfState.backgroundFull:
William Galvin is the Secretary of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts — the state's chief elections officer, corporate regulator, securities watchdog, and records custodian. He was first elected in 1994 and has been re-elected continuously for three decades, making him the longest-serving constitutional officer in Massachusetts history.

Galvin was born in Boston and raised in Brighton. He earned his undergraduate degree and law degree from Boston College. Before becoming Secretary, he served in the Massachusetts House of Representatives for 19 years (1975–1994), including on the powerful House Ways and Means Committee.

The Secretary of State's office covers an unusually broad portfolio. In elections, Galvin's office runs all state and local elections in Massachusetts — including voter registration, ballot design and printing, polling place certification, and election results certification. The office also administers vote-by-mail and early voting programs. In corporate regulation, the office maintains the official registry of all businesses incorporated or registered in Massachusetts, processing more than 200,000 filings annually. In securities regulation, the Securities Division investigates investment fraud and has recovered hundreds of millions of dollars for defrauded Massachusetts investors. The Public Records Division enforces the state's public records law. The office also maintains the Massachusetts Archives, State Library, and Museum of Our National Heritage.

The Secretary's office is the first stop for voter registration, absentee ballot requests, polling place lookups, and election information. The online voter registration portal (sec.state.ma.us/ovr) allows residents to register or update their registration from any device.

sources.label: Office of the Secretary of the Commonwealth — sec.state.ma.us
House.gov — Secretary Galvin Bio Page
Meet Bill — billgalvin.com

treasurer.name: Deborah Goldberg
treasurer.title: State Treasurer
treasurer.office: One Ashburton Place, 12th Floor, Boston, MA 02108
treasurer.background: Deborah Goldberg is the State Treasurer and Receiver-General of Massachusetts, first elected in 2014 and re-elected in 2018 and 2022. She manages the state's cash flow, oversees the Massachusetts State Lottery, and administers college savings and disability savings programs.
treasurer.backgroundFull:
Deborah Goldberg is the State Treasurer and Receiver-General of Massachusetts, first elected in 2014 and re-elected in 2018 and 2022. The Treasurer's office manages the Commonwealth's approximately $9 billion in annual cash flow, oversees the Massachusetts State Lottery, administers the ABLE program for people with disabilities, and serves as co-trustee of the state's pension fund.

Goldberg grew up in Brookline, Massachusetts, attended the University of Vermont and Lesley University, and built a successful career in the family grocery business (Star Markets, later acquired by Shaw's) before entering public service. She has been active in Democratic politics and philanthropy for decades.

As Treasurer, Goldberg has focused on financial empowerment and expanding access to financial products for underserved communities. She has expanded the SaveMA 529 college savings program, created initiatives to bank the unbanked, and reformed lottery operations to modernize the product line while increasing local aid distributions. The Massachusetts State Lottery is consistently among the highest-grossing per-capita lotteries in the world; its net revenues are distributed to municipalities as unrestricted local aid.

Goldberg chairs the Pension Reserves Investment Management (PRIM) board, which oversees approximately $100 billion in assets for state employees and teachers. Under her chairmanship, PRIM has adopted a progressive environmental, social, and governance (ESG) investment framework while maintaining competitive returns.

The Treasurer's unclaimed property program holds billions of dollars belonging to Massachusetts residents — money from forgotten bank accounts, uncashed checks, and insurance policies. Massachusetts residents can search for unclaimed funds at findmassmoney.com.

sources.label: Office of the State Treasurer — mass.gov
Mass.gov — Treasurer Goldberg Official Page
Meet Deb — debgoldberg.com

auditor.name: Diana DiZoglio
auditor.title: State Auditor
auditor.office: One Ashburton Place, Room 1819, Boston, MA 02108
auditor.background: Diana DiZoglio is the State Auditor of Massachusetts, elected in November 2022 and the first woman of Italian descent to hold statewide executive office in the Commonwealth. A former State Representative and Senator, she has made government accountability and transparency her signature issues, including a high-profile effort to audit the Massachusetts Legislature itself.
auditor.backgroundFull:
Diana DiZoglio is the State Auditor of Massachusetts, elected in November 2022 and sworn in on January 19, 2023. She is the first woman of Italian descent to hold statewide executive office in Massachusetts. Before becoming Auditor, she served as State Representative for the 14th Essex District (2013–2019) and State Senator for the First Essex District (2019–2023), representing communities on the North Shore including Methuen, Amesbury, and Salisbury.

DiZoglio was born and raised in Methuen, Massachusetts, in a working-class family, and attended the University of Massachusetts Lowell. As a state legislator, she was known for speaking out on issues that others avoided — including publicly disclosing her own experience with workplace sexual harassment at the State House, helping spur a broader conversation about the legislative workplace culture.

As State Auditor, DiZoglio oversees the Bureau of Special Investigations, which investigates fraud in state programs, and the Bureau of Municipal Finance Law, which assists municipalities with financial management. Her office audits all state agencies, programs, and quasi-public authorities to ensure compliance with law and efficient use of public funds.

DiZoglio attracted national attention for her campaign to audit the Massachusetts State Legislature — a body that has historically claimed exemption from external audit. The initiative went to the Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court, which ultimately ruled that while the Auditor has statutory authority, separation of powers principles limit what the Legislature must disclose. The case raised fundamental questions about legislative accountability that continue to be debated.

DiZoglio's office is a resource for reporting suspected fraud in state programs, including MassHealth fraud, contractor fraud, and misuse of public benefits.

sources.label: Office of the State Auditor — mass.gov
Mass.gov — Auditor DiZoglio Official Page

senators.name: Elizabeth Warren
senators.title: U.S. Senator
senators.office: 317 Russell Senate Office Building, Washington, DC 20510
senators.background: Elizabeth Warren is the senior U.S. Senator from Massachusetts, first elected in 2012 and re-elected in 2018 and 2024. A former Harvard Law professor and architect of the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, she is one of the most prominent progressive legislators in the U.S. Senate.
senators.backgroundFull:
Elizabeth Warren is the senior U.S. Senator from Massachusetts, having first been elected in 2012 when she defeated incumbent Republican Scott Brown, and re-elected in 2018 and 2024. She represents all Massachusetts residents.

Warren was born in Oklahoma City and grew up in Norman, Oklahoma, in a family that experienced economic hardship. She attended the University of Houston on a debate scholarship before transferring to George Washington University (B.S., 1970) and earning her J.D. from Rutgers Law School (1976). She built a distinguished academic career specializing in bankruptcy and commercial law at several leading law schools before receiving tenure at Harvard Law School, where she taught for over 25 years. She became one of the most-cited legal scholars in the country and co-authored the influential book The Two-Income Trap (2003) with her daughter.

Warren first gained national prominence during the 2008 financial crisis as Chair of the Congressional Oversight Panel for TARP, where she publicly held Treasury Secretary Timothy Geithner accountable for how bailout funds were being used. She was the intellectual architect of the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), created by the 2010 Dodd-Frank Act to protect consumers from abusive financial products and practices. The Obama administration opted not to appoint her to lead the CFPB due to anticipated Senate opposition — a slight that helped motivate her Senate run.

In the Senate, Warren serves on the Banking, Housing and Urban Affairs Committee, the Finance Committee, and the Armed Services Committee. She is known for relentless questioning of corporate executives, bank regulators, and administration officials at hearings. In 2020, she ran for the Democratic presidential nomination, dropping out in March after a disappointing Super Tuesday performance. She has been prominently mentioned as a potential Treasury Secretary.

For Massachusetts residents, Senator Warren's Boston office (617-565-3170, 2400 JFK Federal Building) assists with federal agency matters including Social Security, Medicare, VA benefits, USCIS immigration services, and federal contracting. Her website is warren.senate.gov.

sources.label: Official Website — warren.senate.gov
Biographical Directory of Congress — Sen. Warren
Congress.gov — Legislative Activity
GovTrack — Voting Record & Bills
WBUR: Warren wins third Senate term (Nov 2024)

senators.name: Edward Markey
senators.title: U.S. Senator
senators.office: 255 Dirksen Senate Office Building, Washington, DC 20510
senators.background: Edward Markey is the junior U.S. Senator from Massachusetts, having served in the Senate since 2013 and in the U.S. House for 37 years before that (1976–2013). He is best known as the co-author of the Green New Deal resolution and one of the most prolific progressive legislators in the country.
senators.backgroundFull:
Edward Markey is the junior U.S. Senator from Massachusetts, first appointed to fill John Kerry's Senate seat in 2013 when Kerry became Secretary of State, then elected to a full six-year term in 2014, and re-elected in 2020 — defeating a strong primary challenge from Joseph Kennedy III. He previously served in the U.S. House of Representatives for 37 years (1976–2013), representing the 7th and later 5th Congressional Districts covering communities north and west of Boston.

Markey was born and raised in Malden, Massachusetts — a working-class community just north of Boston — in an Irish-American family. He attended Boston College for his undergraduate degree and law degree (J.D., 1972). He served briefly in the U.S. Army Reserve before winning a special election to the Massachusetts House of Representatives in 1973, then running successfully for Congress in 1976 at age 30.

Over his nearly five-decade congressional career, Markey compiled an extraordinary legislative record. His major achievements include the Children's Television Act (1990), which created educational programming requirements for broadcasters; the Telephone Consumer Protection Act (1991), which protects consumers from unwanted robocalls; and landmark energy efficiency standards for appliances that the EPA credits with saving consumers trillions of dollars. He has been a consistent champion of net neutrality, climate action, and nuclear non-proliferation.

In 2019, Markey and Representative Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez introduced the Green New Deal resolution, which brought climate change into the center of the Democratic policy debate and has influenced progressive climate policy nationally. He chairs the Senate Foreign Relations Subcommittee on East Asia, the Pacific, and International Cybersecurity Policy.

For Massachusetts residents, Senator Markey's Boston office (617-565-8519, 975 JFK Federal Building) provides constituent services for Social Security, Medicare, VA benefits, immigration, and federal agency matters. His website is markey.senate.gov.

sources.label: Official Website — markey.senate.gov
Biographical Directory of Congress — Sen. Markey
Congress.gov — Legislative Activity
GovTrack — Voting Record & Bills
WBUR: Markey on his age and 2026 Senate race (Oct 2025)

representative.name: Jake Auchincloss
representative.title: U.S. Representative
representative.office: 1524 Longworth House Office Building, Washington, DC 20515
representative.background: Jake Auchincloss is the U.S. Representative for Massachusetts' 4th Congressional District, which includes Needham along with Newton, Attleboro, Fall River, and communities in Norfolk and Bristol counties. A former Marine Corps officer and Newton City Councilor, he was first elected in 2020.
representative.backgroundFull:
Jake Auchincloss is the U.S. Representative for Massachusetts' 4th Congressional District, representing Needham along with Newton (his hometown), Attleboro, Fall River, and communities across Norfolk and Bristol counties. A Democrat, he was first elected in 2020 in a closely contested nine-way primary and re-elected in 2022 and 2024.

Auchincloss was born and raised in Newton and graduated from Harvard College, where he studied neuroscience and philosophy. He served as a Marine Corps officer, completing three deployments including two to Afghanistan and one to Panama. He earned an M.B.A. from MIT's Sloan School of Management before entering politics. He served on the Newton City Council from 2015 to 2021.

In Congress, Auchincloss serves on the House Financial Services Committee and House Transportation and Infrastructure Committee. He is known for a data-driven, pragmatic approach to policy and has been active on technology and innovation issues, including artificial intelligence regulation, cryptocurrency policy, and federal R&D investment. He has been a strong advocate for the life sciences and biotech industry that is a major employer in his district.

His office assists constituents with federal agency matters including Social Security, Medicare, USCIS immigration services, VA benefits, and federal contracting. He has supported federal investment in Route 128 corridor transportation and has been active on housing affordability legislation.

Auchincloss is one of the youngest members of Congress and has identified himself as a "pragmatic" Democrat willing to work with Republicans on areas of common interest, particularly national security and fiscal policy.

sources.label: Official Congressional Website — auchincloss.house.gov
Biographical Directory of Congress — Rep. Auchincloss
GovTrack — Voting Record & Bills
Congress.gov — Legislative Activity

senator.name: Becca Rausch
senator.title: State Senator
senator.background: Becca Rausch is the Massachusetts State Senator for the Norfolk, Bristol & Middlesex District, representing Needham along with Attleboro, Millis, Milford, Norfolk, North Attleborough, Plainville, Sherborn, Walpole, Wrentham, and surrounding communities. First elected in 2018, she is a leading progressive voice in the Massachusetts Senate.
senator.backgroundFull:
Becca Rausch is the Massachusetts State Senator for the Norfolk, Bristol & Middlesex District, representing a sprawling geographic district that includes Needham along with Attleboro, Millis, Milford, Norfolk, North Attleborough, Plainville, Sherborn, Walpole, Wrentham, and surrounding communities. First elected in 2018, she is one of the leading progressive voices in the Massachusetts Senate.

Rausch is a graduate of Harvard Law School and worked as a civil rights and criminal defense attorney before entering politics. She serves on the Senate Committee on the Judiciary, Senate Ways and Means, and the Joint Committee on the Judiciary. She has been a primary champion of comprehensive voting rights legislation in Massachusetts, including the VOTES Act, which expanded early voting, vote-by-mail, and same-day registration.

She has also been a strong advocate for reproductive rights, criminal justice reform (including legislation to end mandatory minimum sentences for drug offenses), environmental justice, and housing affordability. She is known for her deep engagement with constituents and willingness to hold officials accountable regardless of party.

Her office handles state-level constituent services and can assist with issues including MassHealth, unemployment, housing assistance, and state agency matters. She holds regular office hours throughout the district.

Rausch was re-elected in 2022 and has been mentioned as a rising star in Massachusetts Democratic politics, with her district-wide advocacy on issues from environmental permitting reform to LGBTQ+ protections earning her a significant statewide profile.

sources.label: MA Legislature — Sen. Rausch Official Profile
Sen. Rausch's Official Site — beccarauschma.com
MA Legislature — Sen. Rausch Legislation

representative.name: Joshua Tarsky
representative.title: State Representative
committees: Committee on Climate Action and Sustainability
representative.background: Joshua Tarsky is the Massachusetts State Representative for the 13th Norfolk District, representing Needham, Dover, and part of Medfield. First elected in November 2024, he succeeded retiring Rep. Denise Garlick. A former U.S. Army Special Forces soldier (Green Beret), school principal, and JAG attorney, he brings a distinctive background of military and public service to Beacon Hill.
representative.backgroundFull:
Joshua Tarsky is the Massachusetts State Representative for the 13th Norfolk District, representing the towns of Needham, Dover, and part of Medfield. He was first elected in November 2024, succeeding retiring Representative Denise Garlick who had held the seat since 2011, and was sworn into the 194th General Court in January 2025.

Tarsky's path to the State House is unusually varied. He enrolled at Yale University in 2001 but left after the September 11 attacks, enlisting in the U.S. Army. He served as a Special Forces soldier — a Green Beret — completing two tours in Afghanistan and one in Honduras. After leaving active duty, he earned a graduate degree in education from Harvard University and a law degree from Suffolk University. He has since served as a JAG attorney in the Massachusetts Army National Guard.

Before running for office, Tarsky worked as a public school educator, serving as a teacher in Lawrence and as principal of Holbrook Middle-High School. He lives in Needham with his wife and three children. His blend of military, legal, and educational experience is rare for a freshman legislator.

In the 2024 Democratic primary, Tarsky defeated three opponents to win the nomination for the open seat, carrying nine of Needham's ten precincts. He then won the general election over unenrolled candidate Bill Dermody, continuing the district's tradition of Democratic representation.

Tarsky serves on the House Committee on Climate Action and Sustainability and the Committee on Education. His policy priorities include housing affordability for seniors and working families, public education reform following the 2024 ballot initiative eliminating MCAS as a graduation requirement, and clean energy and transportation improvements. He has been a vocal advocate for banning cell phones in public schools and expanding access to mental health counseling in schools.

His office provides constituent services for state agency matters including MassHealth, the Department of Unemployment Assistance, the RMV, and state housing programs. Constituents can reach his State House office at Joshua.Tarsky@mahouse.gov or (617) 722-2100.

sources.label: MA Legislature — Rep. Tarsky Official Profile
Rep. Tarsky's Official Website
Needham Local: Tarsky wins state rep race (Nov 2024)

mayor.name: Needham Select Board
mayor.title: Select Board (Town Government)
mayor.background: The Needham Select Board is the chief executive body of Needham, Massachusetts, consisting of five members elected for staggered three-year terms. The Board sets town policy, approves the annual budget, and appoints the Town Manager, who handles day-to-day administration.
mayor.backgroundFull:
The Needham Select Board is the chief executive body of Needham, Massachusetts — a prosperous suburban town of approximately 32,000 residents located 12 miles southwest of Boston. The Board consists of five members elected for staggered three-year terms at the Annual Town Election held each April.

As of 2025, the Board includes members Marianne Cooley, Josh Levy, John Keane, Lakshmi Balachandra (elected April 2021, first person of color to serve on the Board), and Marcus Nelson (elected April 2021). The Board appoints the Town Manager, who serves as Needham's chief administrative officer and is responsible for the day-to-day management of all town departments.

The Select Board sets town policy on a wide range of issues including land use and zoning, economic development, public safety, and municipal finance. It approves the annual operating budget (presented to Representative Town Meeting for adoption) and capital improvement plan. The Board also issues licenses and permits including liquor licenses, special event permits, and various business operating licenses.

Needham operates under a Representative Town Meeting form of government, in which 240 elected Town Meeting Members from 10 precincts, plus 14 at-large members, serve as the legislative body. Town Meeting convenes annually (typically in May) and at special sessions as needed. The Select Board and Town Manager present the proposed budget and major policy initiatives to Town Meeting for approval.

The Board meets regularly twice monthly in the Select Board Chambers at Town Hall, 1471 Highland Avenue. All meetings are open to the public. Residents may address the Board during the public comment period at the start of each meeting, or by contacting the Select Board's Office at 781-455-7500 ext. 71204. Needham has consistently earned high marks for fiscal management, receiving bond rating upgrades and recognition as one of the best-managed towns in Massachusetts.

sources.label: Town of Needham — Official Site
Needham Select Board — Meet the Members (2025–2026)

cityCouncil.name: Needham Town Meeting
cityCouncil.title: Representative Town Meeting
cityCouncil.background: Needham's legislative body is Representative Town Meeting, consisting of 240 elected Town Meeting Members from 10 precincts plus 14 at-large members. Town Meeting convenes annually in May to adopt the town budget and enact bylaws.
cityCouncil.backgroundFull:
Needham operates under a Representative Town Meeting (RTM) form of government, in which 240 elected Town Meeting Members from 10 precincts, plus 14 at-large members, serve as the legislative body. Town Meeting convenes annually (typically in May) and in special sessions as needed.

Town Meeting Members are elected to three-year staggered terms in the Annual Town Election held each April. Any registered voter in Needham may run for Town Meeting. The body is presided over by the Town Moderator, who is also elected annually.

Town Meeting's responsibilities include adopting the annual operating and capital budgets, enacting town bylaws and zoning changes, authorizing borrowing, and voting on citizen petitions. The Select Board and Town Manager present the proposed budget and major policy initiatives to Town Meeting for approval. Town Meeting acts as a check on executive authority and gives residents a direct voice in local governance.

Meetings are held at Needham High School's Joyce Middle School auditorium or another large public venue. All sessions are open to the public — any Needham resident may attend and speak during public comment, though only elected Town Meeting Members may vote. Agendas, warrants, and results are posted at needhamma.gov.

sources.label: Town of Needham — Representative Town Meeting
Town of Needham — Official Site

senators.name: Elizabeth Warren
senators.title: U.S. Senator
senators.office: 317 Russell Senate Office Building, Washington, DC 20510

A former Harvard Law professor and architect of the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, she is one of the most prominent progressive legislators in the U.S. Senate.

senators.background: Edward Markey is the junior U.S. Senator from Massachusetts, having served in the Senate since 2013 and in the U.S. House for 37 years before that (1976–2013). He is best known as the co-author of the Green New Deal resolution and one of the most prolific progressive legislators in the country.

representative.name: Katherine Clark
representative.title: U.S. Representative
representative.office: 2448 Rayburn House Office Building, Washington, DC 20515
representative.background: Katherine Clark is the U.S. Representative for Massachusetts' 5th Congressional District and serves as the House Democratic Whip — the second-highest leadership position in the House Democratic caucus. She represents Waltham, Newton, Framingham, Malden, and surrounding communities.
representative.backgroundFull:
Katherine Clark is the U.S. Representative for Massachusetts' 5th Congressional District and serves as the House Democratic Whip — the second-highest ranking position in the House Democratic caucus, making her one of the most powerful Democrats in Congress. She represents Waltham, Newton, Framingham, Malden, Medford, Melrose, Somerville (part), and surrounding communities.

Clark was born in New Haven, Connecticut, and earned her undergraduate degree from Saint Lawrence University, her J.D. from Cornell Law School, and a Master of Public Administration from Harvard's Kennedy School. Before Congress, she served as a member of the Massachusetts House of Representatives (2008–2011), a Massachusetts State Senator (2011–2013), and was first elected to Congress in a 2013 special election.

As House Democratic Whip, Clark is responsible for maintaining party unity on key votes and communicating the Democratic caucus's legislative priorities. She has been a leading voice in Congress on issues including childcare affordability, paid family and medical leave, the opioid crisis, and LGBTQ+ rights. She was a key architect of the American Rescue Plan Act (2021) and has consistently advocated for stronger federal investment in public education.

The district includes major employers such as Raytheon Technologies, Brandeis University, and numerous biotech and life sciences firms. She has championed federal funding for Route 128 corridor transportation improvements and supported workforce development initiatives for the region's technology sector.

sources.label: Official Congressional Website — katherineclark.house.gov
Biographical Directory of Congress — Rep. Clark
GovTrack — Voting Record & Bills
Congress.gov — Legislative Activity

senator.name: Michael J. Barrett
senator.title: State Senator
committees: Committee on Telecommunications, Utilities and Energy (Chair)
senator.background: Michael J. Barrett is the Massachusetts State Senator for the 3rd Middlesex District, representing Waltham, Bedford, Carlisle, Chelmsford, Concord, Lincoln, Weston, and part of Lexington. First elected in 2012, he is the chief architect of three landmark Massachusetts climate laws and serves as Senate Chair of the Committee on Telecommunications, Utilities and Energy.
senator.backgroundFull:
Michael J. Barrett is the Massachusetts State Senator for the 3rd Middlesex District, representing the city of Waltham and the towns of Bedford, Carlisle, Chelmsford, Concord, Lincoln, Weston, and part of Lexington. He was first elected in 2012 and re-elected in 2014, 2016, 2018, 2020, 2022, and 2024.

Barrett was born in Agana, Guam, and grew up in Reading, Massachusetts. He graduated from Reading High School in 1966, Harvard College magna cum laude in 1970, and Northeastern University School of Law in 1977, after which he clerked for a U.S. District Court judge. He has an unusually long political career spanning four decades: he first served in the Massachusetts House (1979–1985), then the State Senate (1987–1994) representing a different district. He departed politics for 16 years, working as CEO and General Counsel of the Visiting Nurse Associations of New England and later as a health care IT analyst. He returned to politics in 2012, winning the 3rd Middlesex Senate seat.

Barrett's signature achievement in his current tenure is serving as the chief Senate architect of three major climate bills: An Act Creating a Next Generation Roadmap for Massachusetts Climate Policy (2021), An Act Driving Clean Energy and Offshore Wind (2022), and An Act Promoting a Clean Energy Grid, Advancing Equity and Protecting Ratepayers (2024). Each was a sweeping, multi-section law addressing energy infrastructure, emissions targets, offshore wind, solar, EV charging, and ratepayer protections. He serves as Senate Chair of the Committee on Telecommunications, Utilities and Energy.

Barrett has also championed legislation to open the records of individuals who lived and died in Massachusetts state institutions for people with intellectual and developmental disabilities, including the Fernald School in Waltham — a cause that reflects his district's local history as well as his longstanding commitment to disability rights.

Earlier in his career, Barrett authored a 1990 Atlantic Monthly cover story advocating a longer school day and year — a piece that led Congress to create the National Education Commission on Time and Learning, of which he was a member. In 1992 he drafted Massachusetts' first-in-the-nation domestic violence registry law, and in 1989 led passage of the state's first law barring discrimination based on sexual orientation.

His office handles constituent services for all state agency matters. Constituents can reach Senator Barrett at Michael.Barrett@masenate.gov or (617) 722-1572.

sources.label: MA Legislature — Sen. Barrett Official Profile
Sen. Barrett's Official Website — senatormikebarrett.com
Sen. Barrett's Bio:senatormikebarrett.com

representative.name: Thomas M. Stanley
representative.title: State Representative
representative.background: Thomas M. Stanley is the Massachusetts State Representative for the 9th Middlesex District, representing the majority of Waltham. First elected in 2008, he is a longtime Waltham City Councilor who has served in both bodies simultaneously, making him one of the most entrenched local legislators in the region. He chairs the House Committee on Aging and Independence.
representative.backgroundFull:
Thomas M. Stanley is the Massachusetts State Representative for the 9th Middlesex District, which covers the majority of Waltham. He was first elected to the State House in 2008 and has been re-elected continuously since, most recently winning re-election in November 2024. He is also a long-serving member of the Waltham City Council, having served as an at-large councilor simultaneously with his legislative duties — a dual role that reflects his deep roots in Waltham civic life. In April 2025, he announced he would not seek re-election to the City Council, choosing to focus on his State House responsibilities amid looming federal budget challenges.

Stanley's 9th Middlesex District encompasses most of Waltham's wards, making him the primary Beacon Hill voice for the majority of Waltham residents. He is a member of the American Society for Public Administrators, the Friends of Waltham Library, the Hardy Pond Association, and the Massachusetts Government Finance Officers Association Fiscal Policy Committee.

In the legislature, Stanley serves as Chair of the House Committee on Aging and Independence — a role he has embraced with particular urgency given concerns about proposed federal cuts to Medicaid and the Department of Health and Human Services. He has been an outspoken critic of federal policies he believes would harm Massachusetts seniors and vulnerable residents, calling potential Medicaid cuts "catastrophic" for the state.

Stanley has supported investments in regional transportation infrastructure, including improvements to commuter rail and Route 128 highway access that are critical to Waltham's large employer base. He has advocated for increased Chapter 70 school aid, opioid epidemic response, and housing affordability legislation.

His office provides constituent services for state agency matters including MassHealth, the Division of Unemployment Assistance, the RMV, and state housing programs. Constituents can reach him at Thomas.Stanley@mahouse.gov or (617) 722-2230.

sources.label: MA Legislature — Rep. Stanley Official Profile
Rep. Stanley's Official Website — tomstanley.org
Waltham Times: Stanley not seeking City Council re-election (Apr 2025)

mayor.name: Jeannette McCarthy
mayor.title: Mayor
mayor.background: Jeannette A. McCarthy is the longest-serving Mayor in the history of Waltham, Massachusetts, having first been elected in 2003 and beginning her sixth term in 2024. She is the only mayor in Waltham's history to have served at all three levels of local government.
mayor.backgroundFull:
Jeannette A. McCarthy is the longest-serving Mayor in the history of Waltham, Massachusetts, having first been elected in 2003 and beginning her sixth term in 2024. A lifelong Waltham resident, she attended Waltham public schools — Hardy Elementary, North Junior High, and Waltham High School (Class of 1971) — before earning a B.S. in Biology magna cum laude from Boston College (1975), a J.D. cum laude from Suffolk University Law School (1983), and an M.S. in Criminal Justice from Northeastern University (1986).

Before becoming mayor, McCarthy served on the Waltham School Committee (1986–1991), worked for seven years as City Solicitor and Assistant City Solicitor in the Waltham Law Department (1992–2000), and served two years as a City Councilor-at-Large (2002–2004). She is the only mayor in the city's history to have served at all three levels of local government: School Committee, City Council, and Mayor.

During her tenure, McCarthy has overseen the construction of the new Waltham High School (completed 2024), the acquisition of approximately 300 acres of open space, the revitalization of Moody Street as a pedestrian-friendly dining and cultural corridor, and more than $6 million in funding secured for the Waltham Housing Authority. Her administration completed the renovation of the Moody Street Fire Station and initiated construction of a new Police Station. She has been a consistent opponent of overdevelopment, prioritizing neighborhood character and infrastructure quality over developer profits.

McCarthy has been recognized for her deep community ties and pro bono legal work, serving as advisor to numerous Waltham-based nonprofits and scholarship funds over three decades. She is known for an approach to city government that prioritizes residents above political expediency, a record underscored by her lopsided electoral victories in recent cycles.

sources.label: City of Waltham — Official Site
City of Waltham — Mayor's Office
City of Waltham: About Mayor
Jeannette for Mayor — Official Campaign Site

cityCouncil.name: Waltham City Council
cityCouncil.title: City Council
cityCouncil.background: The Waltham City Council is the 15-member legislative body of Waltham, consisting of 6 councillors-at-large and 9 ward councillors. Council President: Robert G. Logan (Ward 9). The Council works alongside Mayor Jeannette McCarthy to adopt the city budget, enact ordinances, and approve zoning changes.
cityCouncil.backgroundFull:
The Waltham City Council is the legislative body of Waltham, Massachusetts, consisting of 15 members: 6 councillors-at-large and 9 ward councillors. All members are elected to two-year terms. The Council works alongside Mayor Jeannette McCarthy to adopt the annual budget, enact ordinances, approve zoning changes, and confirm mayoral appointments. Meetings are open to the public at Waltham City Hall, 610 Main Street.

additionalLocals.name: John J. Lawn Jr.
additionalLocals.title: State Representative
committees: Joint Committee on Health Care Financing (House Chair)
additionalLocals.background: John J. Lawn Jr. is the Massachusetts State Representative for the 10th Middlesex District, representing portions of Waltham along with parts of Newton and Watertown. First elected in 2011, he is House Chair of the Joint Committee on Health Care Financing and has been a long-serving advocate for disability rights, health care access, and local services.
additionalLocals.backgroundFull:
John J. Lawn Jr. is the Massachusetts State Representative for the 10th Middlesex District, which covers specific wards and precincts of Waltham (Ward 1 Precincts 1, 2A, 4; Ward 2 Precinct 1A; Ward 3 Precincts 1A and 4; Ward 4 Precincts 1A, 2; Ward 5 Precincts 1, 2A; Ward 6 Precinct 2; Ward 8 Precinct 2; and Ward 9), along with portions of Newton and Watertown. He has represented this district since May 2011, winning a special election to fill the seat.

Note: Waltham is served by two State Representatives. Thomas M. Stanley (9th Middlesex) covers the remaining wards and precincts of the city. Residents should confirm which district applies to their specific address using the Massachusetts Legislature's district finder at malegislature.gov.

Lawn was born and raised in Watertown, Massachusetts. He earned a degree in political science and sociology from Merrimack College and has worked as the owner of Charles River Real Estate. He previously served as a District Councilor in Watertown before winning his State House seat.

Lawn serves as House Chair of the Joint Committee on Health Care Financing — one of the most consequential committees in the legislature, with jurisdiction over MassHealth, the state's Medicaid program, health insurance regulations, and the health care delivery system. He has been a consistent advocate for disability rights and employment, earning recognition from the Massachusetts Commission on the Status of Persons with Disabilities. He has championed legislation such as Operation House Call and has long supported programs that enable people with intellectual and developmental disabilities to participate fully in the workforce.

In July 2025, Lawn appeared in Boston Municipal Court and pleaded guilty to operating a vehicle under the influence of alcohol and leaving the scene of property damage following an early-morning incident in Boston. He received one year of probation and a 45-day license suspension. He has continued to serve in his legislative role.

Constituents in the 10th Middlesex portion of Waltham can reach Rep. Lawn's office at John.Lawn@mahouse.gov or (617) 722-2400.

sources.label: MA Legislature — Rep. Lawn Official Profile
Waltham Times: Lawn charged with OUI (Jul 2025)
Watertown News: Lawn honored as Champion of Youth (Mar 2025)

senators.name: Elizabeth Warren
senators.title: U.S. Senator
senators.office: 317 Russell Senate Office Building, Washington, DC 20510
senators.background: Elizabeth Warren is the senior U.S. Senator from Massachusetts, first elected in 2012 and re-elected in 2018 and 2024. A former Harvard Law professor and architect of the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, she is one of the most prominent progressive legislators in the U.S. Senate.
senators.backgroundFull:
Elizabeth Warren is the senior U.S. Senator from Massachusetts, having first been elected in 2012 when she defeated incumbent Republican Scott Brown, and re-elected in 2018 and 2024. She represents all Massachusetts residents.

Warren was born in Oklahoma City and grew up in Norman, Oklahoma, in a family that experienced economic hardship. She attended the University of Houston on a debate scholarship before transferring to George Washington University (B.S., 1970) and earning her J.D. from Rutgers Law School (1976). She built a distinguished academic career specializing in bankruptcy and commercial law at several leading law schools before receiving tenure at Harvard Law School, where she taught for over 25 years. She became one of the most-cited legal scholars in the country and co-authored the influential book The Two-Income Trap (2003) with her daughter.

Warren first gained national prominence during the 2008 financial crisis as Chair of the Congressional Oversight Panel for TARP, where she publicly held Treasury Secretary Timothy Geithner accountable for how bailout funds were being used. She was the intellectual architect of the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), created by the 2010 Dodd-Frank Act to protect consumers from abusive financial products and practices. The Obama administration opted not to appoint her to lead the CFPB due to anticipated Senate opposition — a slight that helped motivate her Senate run.

In the Senate, Warren serves on the Banking, Housing and Urban Affairs Committee, the Finance Committee, and the Armed Services Committee. She is known for relentless questioning of corporate executives, bank regulators, and administration officials at hearings. In 2020, she ran for the Democratic presidential nomination, dropping out in March after a disappointing Super Tuesday performance. She has been prominently mentioned as a potential Treasury Secretary.

For Massachusetts residents, Senator Warren's Boston office (617-565-3170, 2400 JFK Federal Building) assists with federal agency matters including Social Security, Medicare, VA benefits, USCIS immigration services, and federal contracting. Her website is warren.senate.gov.

sources.label: Official Website — warren.senate.gov
WBUR: Warren wins third Senate term (Nov 2024)
WBUR: Warren on election results and Senate minority (Nov 2024)
WBUR: Warren re-election announcement (Mar 2023)
WBUR: Warren looks back at presidential bid (May 2021)
WBUR: MA Democrats plan for minority status (Jan 2025)

senators.name: Edward Markey
senators.title: U.S. Senator
senators.office: 255 Dirksen Senate Office Building, Washington, DC 20510
senators.background: Edward Markey is the junior U.S. Senator from Massachusetts, having served in the Senate since 2013 and in the U.S. House for 37 years before that (1976–2013). He is best known as the co-author of the Green New Deal resolution and one of the most prolific progressive legislators in the country.
senators.backgroundFull:
Edward Markey is the junior U.S. Senator from Massachusetts, first appointed to fill John Kerry's Senate seat in 2013 when Kerry became Secretary of State, then elected to a full six-year term in 2014, and re-elected in 2020 — defeating a strong primary challenge from Joseph Kennedy III. He previously served in the U.S. House of Representatives for 37 years (1976–2013), representing the 7th and later 5th Congressional Districts covering communities north and west of Boston.

Markey was born and raised in Malden, Massachusetts — a working-class community just north of Boston — in an Irish-American family. He attended Boston College for his undergraduate degree and law degree (J.D., 1972). He served briefly in the U.S. Army Reserve before winning a special election to the Massachusetts House of Representatives in 1973, then running successfully for Congress in 1976 at age 30.

Over his nearly five-decade congressional career, Markey compiled an extraordinary legislative record. His major achievements include the Children's Television Act (1990), which created educational programming requirements for broadcasters; the Telephone Consumer Protection Act (1991), which protects consumers from unwanted robocalls; and landmark energy efficiency standards for appliances that the EPA credits with saving consumers trillions of dollars. He has been a consistent champion of net neutrality, climate action, and nuclear non-proliferation.

In 2019, Markey and Representative Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez introduced the Green New Deal resolution, which brought climate change into the center of the Democratic policy debate and has influenced progressive climate policy nationally. He chairs the Senate Foreign Relations Subcommittee on East Asia, the Pacific, and International Cybersecurity Policy.

For Massachusetts residents, Senator Markey's Boston office (617-565-8519, 975 JFK Federal Building) provides constituent services for Social Security, Medicare, VA benefits, immigration, and federal agency matters. His website is markey.senate.gov.

sources.label: Official Website — markey.senate.gov
WBUR: Markey on his age and 2026 Senate race (Oct 2025)
WBUR: Markey on Green New Deal with AOC (Feb 2019)
WBUR: Markey final pitch to MA voters (Aug 2020)
WBUR: MA Democrats plan for minority status (Jan 2025)

representative.name: Stephen Lynch
representative.title: U.S. Representative
representative.office: 2109 Rayburn House Office Building, Washington, DC 20515
representative.background: Stephen F. Lynch is the U.S. Representative for Massachusetts' 8th Congressional District, which includes Quincy, South Boston, parts of Dorchester, Brockton, Plymouth, and communities on the South Shore. First elected in 2001, he is one of the most senior members of the Massachusetts delegation.
representative.backgroundFull:
Stephen F. Lynch is the U.S. Representative for Massachusetts' 8th Congressional District, representing Quincy, South Boston, Dorchester (part), Brockton, Plymouth, and communities along the South Shore and Cape Cod. First elected in 2001 in a special election, he is the most senior member of the Massachusetts congressional delegation and one of the few Democrats in Congress who regularly breaks with his party on certain votes.

Lynch was born in South Boston to a working-class Irish-American family and worked as a structural ironworker before attending Wentworth Institute of Technology for engineering. He later earned a J.D. from Boston College Law School and an M.A. from Harvard's Kennedy School. He served in the Massachusetts House and Senate before coming to Congress.

Lynch is known for his moderate-to-conservative positions on some social issues — he was one of the few House Democrats to oppose the Affordable Care Act in 2010 — while being strongly pro-labor and pro-worker economically. He is a member of the House Financial Services Committee and the House Oversight and Reform Committee, where he has been an active investigator of financial industry misconduct and federal contracting waste.

His office provides assistance with federal agencies including Social Security, Medicare, the VA, USCIS (immigration), and federal small business programs. He has been a consistent advocate for South Shore transportation, supporting the Red Line and commuter rail, and has worked on flood mitigation funding for the district's coastal communities.

Lynch serves on the Congressional Steel Caucus and the Congressional Shipbuilding Caucus, reflecting the industrial and maritime heritage of his district.

sources.label: Official Congressional Website — lynch.house.gov
Biographical Directory of Congress — Rep. Lynch
GovTrack — Voting Record & Bills
Congress.gov — Legislative Activity

senator.name: John F. Keenan
senator.title: State Senator
committees: Committee on Mental Health, Substance Use and Recovery
senator.background: John F. Keenan is the Massachusetts State Senator for the Norfolk & Plymouth District, representing Quincy along with Abington, Braintree, Hanover, Holbrook, and Rockland. First elected in 2010 and currently serving his seventh term, he is Senate Chair of the Committee on Election Laws and a leading voice on public health and behavioral health policy.
senator.backgroundFull:
John F. Keenan is the Massachusetts State Senator for the Norfolk & Plymouth District, representing the city of Quincy and the towns of Abington, Braintree, Hanover, Holbrook, and Rockland, as well as precincts 3B, 4B, 6A, and 6B1 in Braintree. He was first elected in 2010, succeeding Brian Joyce, and has been re-elected continuously since — currently in his seventh term.

Keenan serves as Senate Chair of the Committee on Election Laws, which has jurisdiction over voter registration, campaign finance, and election administration. He also serves on the Committee on Public Health and the Committee on Mental Health, Substance Use and Recovery, reflecting his longtime focus on health care issues.

Throughout his tenure, Keenan has been particularly focused on public and behavioral health — working to expand access to mental health services, address the opioid epidemic, and strengthen community health systems across his district. He has described these as the central themes of his legislative work, emphasizing support for the Commonwealth's most vulnerable residents.

Keenan has been a consistent advocate for South Shore transportation, including improvements to the Red Line (which serves Quincy heavily), commuter rail, and Route 3 and Route 3A corridor maintenance. He has also worked on coastal resiliency and flood mitigation funding for communities along the South Shore, including Quincy's waterfront neighborhoods, which have faced repeated major flooding.

He has supported local aid increases for cities and towns in his district, worked to direct state economic development resources to Quincy and the South Shore, and has been active on legislation related to veterans' services, seniors, and housing affordability.

His office handles constituent services for all state agency matters including MassHealth, the Division of Unemployment Assistance, DTA, the EOEA, and state housing programs. Constituents can reach Senator Keenan at John.Keenan@masenate.gov or (617) 722-1494.

sources.label: MA Legislature — Sen. Keenan Official Profile
Sen. Keenan's Official Website — senatorjohnkeenan.com
City of Quincy — State Delegation Page

representative.name: Tackey Chan
representative.title: State Representative
committees: Committee on Consumer Protection and Professional Licensure (House Chair)
representative.background: Tackey Chan is the Massachusetts State Representative for the 2nd Norfolk District, representing portions of Quincy. First elected in 2010 and currently serving his seventh term, he is House Chair of the Joint Committee on Consumer Protection and Professional Licensure and one of the most senior legislators representing Quincy on Beacon Hill.
representative.backgroundFull:
Tackey Chan is the Massachusetts State Representative for the 2nd Norfolk District, which covers portions of Quincy including Ward 1, parts of Wards 3, 4, and 5 — encompassing the neighborhoods of Houghs Neck, Germantown, Adams Shore, Quincy Center, and parts of Wollaston. He was first elected in 2010 and is currently serving his seventh term, making him one of the most experienced legislators from Quincy.

Chan serves as House Chair of the Joint Committee on Consumer Protection and Professional Licensure, a committee with jurisdiction over a wide range of professional licensing boards, consumer fraud issues, and regulatory matters affecting Massachusetts residents and businesses.

During his tenure, Chan has been a consistent advocate for his Quincy constituents on issues including transportation (the Red Line, MBTA bus service, and South Shore Commuter Rail), housing affordability, small business support, and the opioid epidemic. Quincy's 2nd Norfolk District includes a diverse and growing community with significant Asian-American populations in several neighborhoods, and Chan — himself of Chinese-American heritage — has been an advocate for immigrant and minority communities on Beacon Hill.

Chan has worked to direct state resources to Quincy including Chapter 70 school funding, Chapter 90 highway funds, and state economic development grants. He regularly assists constituents navigating state agencies including MassHealth, the Division of Unemployment Assistance, the RMV, and the Department of Children and Families.

Note: Quincy is served by two state representatives — Tackey Chan (2nd Norfolk) and Bruce J. Ayers (1st Norfolk) — reflecting the city's size and population. Ward 3 Precincts 3–5, Ward 4 Precincts 1 and 3, and Ward 6 are represented by Rep. Ayers.

Constituents can reach Rep. Chan's office at Tackey.Chan@mahouse.gov or (617) 722-2370.

sources.label: MA Legislature — Rep. Chan Official Profile
Rep. Chan's Official Website — tackeychan.org
City of Quincy — State Delegation Page

mayor.name: Thomas Koch
mayor.title: Mayor
mayor.background: Thomas P. Koch is the 33rd Mayor of Quincy, Massachusetts, and the longest-serving mayor in the city's history. First sworn in on January 7, 2008, he is currently serving his seventh term. Koch was born and raised in Quincy and has dedicated his career entirely to public service in his hometown.
mayor.backgroundFull:
Thomas P. Koch is the 33rd Mayor of Quincy, Massachusetts, and the longest-serving mayor in the city's history. Born and raised in Quincy as the sixth of seven children of Richard and Simone Koch, he graduated from North Quincy High School in 1981 (class president) and spent approximately 20 years in various leadership positions within city government — including as Executive Director of the Parks and Forestry Department — before launching a successful campaign for mayor in 2007.

First sworn in on January 7, 2008, Koch has been re-elected six times. His administration has overseen a dramatic transformation of Quincy: the construction of three new state-of-the-art school buildings, replacement of aging water and sewer infrastructure, construction of seawalls and flood mitigation systems in response to major flooding events in 2010 and 2018, and a wholesale revitalization of downtown Quincy featuring new residential developments, restaurants, and nationally recognized public spaces including the Hancock-Adams Common.

Koch has also expanded Quincy's fire department to four ladder companies for the first time since 1989 and has pursued major road and sidewalk improvements citywide. Under his tenure, Quincy's population has grown from approximately 92,000 to over 100,000, and the city has earned recognition from publications including Boston Magazine, the Wall Street Journal, and the New York Times. Quincy has received bond rating upgrades from Wall Street during his tenure.

Koch's administration has not been without controversy. In 2024, his proposal to raise his salary from $159,000 to $285,000 drew public backlash and was deferred. In 2025, his unilateral decision to commission $850,000 in religious statues for the city's new public safety building prompted litigation and a state court injunction. He is a family man who married his high school sweetheart Christine Keenan in 1990; they have three children and live near his childhood home in North Quincy.

sources.label: City of Quincy — Mayor's Official Bio
WBUR: Koch in 7th term, quelling controversies (Nov 2025)
Koch for Quincy — Meet the Mayor

cityCouncil.name: Quincy City Council
cityCouncil.title: City Council
cityCouncil.background: The Quincy City Council is the legislative body of Quincy, Massachusetts, consisting of 9 members — 6 ward councilors representing each of Quincy's 6 wards, and 3 councilors-at-large. The Council works alongside Mayor Thomas Koch to set city policy, adopt the annual budget, and enact local ordinances.
cityCouncil.backgroundFull:
The Quincy City Council is the legislative body of Quincy, Massachusetts. It consists of 9 members: 6 ward councilors (one for each of Quincy's six wards — Ward 1 through Ward 6) and 3 councilors-at-large who represent the entire city. All members are elected to two-year terms.

The City Council works alongside Mayor Thomas Koch to govern Quincy. The Council's responsibilities include adopting the annual municipal budget, enacting local ordinances and zoning changes, approving major contracts and expenditures, and confirming certain mayoral appointments. The Council also holds regular public hearings on zoning variance requests and development projects.

Quincy's six wards cover distinct geographic areas of the city: Ward 1 (North Quincy, Wollaston), Ward 2 (Quincy Center, Merrymount), Ward 3 (Quincy Point, Marina Bay area), Ward 4 (West Quincy, Squantum), Ward 5 (South Quincy, Germantown), and Ward 6 (Montclair, Adams Shore, Atlantic area).

City Council meetings are held at Quincy City Hall, 1305 Hancock Street, and are open to the public. Residents may address the Council during public comment periods. To contact your ward councilor or an at-large councilor, visit the City of Quincy website at quincyma.gov or call City Hall at 617-376-1000.

sources.label: City of Quincy — City Council Official Page
WBUR: Koch allies suffer big council election losses (Nov 2025)
WATD: Voters oust four incumbent Quincy councilors (Nov 2025)

additionalLocals.name: Bruce J. Ayers
additionalLocals.title: State Representative
committees: Joint Committee on Community Development and Small Business
additionalLocals.background: Bruce J. Ayers is the Massachusetts State Representative for the 1st Norfolk District, representing portions of Quincy (Ward 3 Precincts 3–5, Ward 4 Precincts 1 & 3, and Ward 6) and parts of Randolph. First elected in 1999, he is one of the longest-serving legislators representing Quincy and has been a consistent champion for people with disabilities, small businesses, and senior services.
additionalLocals.backgroundFull:
Bruce J. Ayers is the Massachusetts State Representative for the 1st Norfolk District, covering Ward 3 Precincts 3, 4, and 5, Ward 4 Precincts 1 and 3, and Ward 6 of Quincy, as well as Precincts 7, 8, 11, and 12 of Randolph. He has represented this district since 1999, making him one of the longest-serving state legislators from Quincy.

Ayers was born on April 17, 1962, in Boston and grew up in Quincy, graduating from North Quincy High School. He attended Northeastern University and the Harvard University Extension School, and completed the Boston College Small Business Development Program. He owns Ayers Handicap Conversion Center, a business he has operated for over 35 years that retrofits vehicles with adaptive driving equipment for people with physical disabilities. This professional background has made disability rights and accessibility one of the cornerstones of his legislative work.

Before his election to the State House, Ayers served on the Quincy Democratic City Committee from 1990 to 2000 and was elected to the Quincy City Council for Ward 6 in 1992, serving there until his election to the legislature in 1999.

In the legislature, Ayers has been a steady advocate for the physically disabled, filing legislation to strengthen enforcement against illegal use of handicapped parking spaces. He has also worked on animal welfare legislation, senior services, and small business support. He hosts a regular television program, Legislative Update with Rep. Bruce Ayers, on Quincy public access TV, where he discusses legislation and interviews local officials — a reflection of his focus on constituent communication and transparency.

Note: Quincy is served by three State Representatives. Bruce Ayers covers the 1st Norfolk (Ward 3 Precincts 3–5, Ward 4 Precincts 1 & 3, Ward 6), Tackey Chan covers the 2nd Norfolk, and Ronald Mariano (House Speaker) covers the 3rd Norfolk. Residents should confirm their district at malegislature.gov/Search/FindMyLegislator.

Constituents can reach Rep. Ayers at Bruce.Ayers@mahouse.gov or (617) 722-2220.

sources.label: MA Legislature — Rep. Ayers Official Profile
Rep. Ayers' Official Website — repbruceayers.com
City of Quincy — State Delegation Page

additionalLocals.name: Ronald J. Mariano
additionalLocals.title: State Representative & Speaker of the House
additionalLocals.office: Speaker of the Massachusetts House of Representatives
additionalLocals.background: Ronald J. Mariano is the Speaker of the Massachusetts House of Representatives and State Representative for the 3rd Norfolk District, representing portions of Quincy, Weymouth, and Holbrook. A lifelong Quincy Point resident, he has served in the House since 1991 and was elected Speaker in December 2020. He was a key architect of Massachusetts' landmark 2006 health care reform law that served as a model for the Affordable Care Act.
additionalLocals.backgroundFull:
Ronald J. Mariano is the Speaker of the Massachusetts House of Representatives — the most powerful position in the state legislature — and the State Representative for the 3rd Norfolk District, covering Ward 2, Ward 4 Precinct 5, and Ward 5 Precinct 1A of Quincy, along with portions of Weymouth and Holbrook. He has served in the Massachusetts House since 1991 and was elected Speaker in December 2020, re-elected to the Speakership in January 2023 and January 2025.

Mariano was born in 1946 in Quincy and is a lifelong resident of Quincy Point. He attended Quincy Public Schools, earned a B.S. from Northeastern University, and an M.Ed. from the University of Massachusetts Boston. He taught in the Quincy Public Schools for 12 years and served on the Quincy School Committee for 18 years before his election to the House in 1991.

Over more than three decades in the legislature, Mariano rose through the ranks from backbencher to one of the most consequential figures in Massachusetts government. He served as Assistant Majority Leader and then Majority Leader from 2011 until his elevation to Speaker. As Speaker he oversees all House operations, sets the legislative agenda, appoints committee chairs, and controls the flow of legislation through the chamber.

Mariano's most significant legislative achievement is his role as House Chair of the Joint Committee on Financial Services and chief architect of the Massachusetts health care reform law of 2006 — the first in the nation to achieve near-universal coverage, and the model upon which the federal Affordable Care Act was based. President Obama has publicly credited Massachusetts' law as the template for the ACA. Mariano was also the lead House negotiator on the 2014 gun safety reform and the subsequent ban on bump stocks, making Massachusetts a national leader on firearms regulation.

As Speaker, Mariano oversaw the disbursement of more than $5 billion in American Rescue Plan Act (ARPA) funds to Massachusetts workers, businesses, and institutions during and after the COVID-19 pandemic. He has championed affordable housing legislation, life sciences investment, clean energy, and LGBTQ+ and reproductive rights.

Quincy residents in the 3rd Norfolk should contact the Speaker's office at Ronald.Mariano@mahouse.gov or (617) 722-2500. All Quincy residents benefit from having the Speaker of the House as one of their three state representatives — a position of enormous influence over the state budget and legislative priorities.

sources.label: MA Legislature — Speaker Mariano Official Profile
Speaker Mariano's Official Website — ronmariano.com
City of Quincy — State Delegation Page

additionalLocals.name: Daniel J. Hunt
additionalLocals.title: State Representative
committees: Committee on Environment and Natural Resources
Committee on Telecommunications, Utilities and Energy
additionalLocals.background: Daniel J. Hunt is the Massachusetts State Representative for the 13th Suffolk District, representing multiple Boston neighborhoods (Dorchester, Roslindale, West Roxbury) and a small portion of Quincy (Ward 3 Precinct 3). A Dorchester native and Boston College and Suffolk Law graduate, he has served since 2015 and is known for his work on environmental policy and constituent services.
additionalLocals.backgroundFull:
Daniel J. Hunt is the Massachusetts State Representative for the 13th Suffolk District. His district is centered in Boston, covering Ward 7 Precinct 9, Ward 13 Precincts 6–10, Ward 15 Precinct 6, Ward 16 Precincts 1–7, 9, 10, and 12, and Ward 17 Precincts 3 and 5 — as well as Quincy Ward 3 Precinct 3 in Norfolk County.

Note: Rep. Hunt represents only one precinct within Quincy (Ward 3 Precinct 3). The majority of Quincy is served by Tackey Chan (2nd Norfolk), Bruce J. Ayers (1st Norfolk), and Ronald Mariano (3rd Norfolk). Quincy residents should confirm their district at malegislature.gov.

Hunt was born and raised in Dorchester, graduating from Boston Latin Academy in 1999. He earned a B.S. in Business and Management Economics from Boston College's Carroll School of Management and a J.D. from Suffolk University Law School. Before his election he served as chief of staff for Representative Daniel Bosley (2008–2011), worked on the John Kerry 2004 presidential campaign, and served as Director of Governmental Affairs at the Massachusetts Department of Conservation and Recreation.

Hunt was elected to the State House in 2014, succeeding Marty Walsh who had just been elected Mayor of Boston. He sits on the Committee on Environment and Natural Resources and the Committee on Telecommunications, Utilities and Energy. He has focused his work on parks, open space, environmental justice, and economic development for his urban district.

Constituents in Quincy Ward 3 Precinct 3 can reach Rep. Hunt at Daniel.Hunt@mahouse.gov or (617) 722-2460.

sources.label: MA Legislature — Rep. Hunt Official Profile
Rep. Hunt's Official Website — danhunt.org

president.name: Donald J. Trump
president.title: President of the United States
president.background: 47th President of the United States, serving his second term beginning January 20, 2025. Former businessman and television personality. 45th President 2017–2021.
president.backgroundFull:
Donald J. Trump is the 47th President of the United States, having been inaugurated on January 20, 2025, following his victory in the November 2024 presidential election over Democratic candidate Kamala Harris. He previously served as the 45th President from January 20, 2017 to January 20, 2021.

Trump was born on June 14, 1946, in Queens, New York City, the fourth of five children of Fred Trump, a real estate developer, and Mary Anne MacLeod Trump. He attended the New York Military Academy and transferred to the Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania, graduating in 1968 with a degree in economics.

Before entering politics, Trump built a career in real estate and entertainment. He expanded his father's real estate company into Manhattan, developed properties including Trump Tower (1983) and a range of hotels and golf courses internationally, and hosted the NBC reality television series The Apprentice from 2004 to 2015. He filed for corporate bankruptcy six times between 1991 and 2009 relating to his casino and hotel businesses in Atlantic City, New Jersey.

Trump announced his first presidential campaign in June 2015 and won the Republican nomination before defeating Democratic nominee Hillary Clinton in the November 2016 general election. His first term was marked by major tax legislation (Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, 2017), the appointment of three Supreme Court justices, renegotiation of the USMCA trade agreement, and the COVID-19 pandemic response. He was impeached twice by the House of Representatives — in December 2019 for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress, and in January 2021 for incitement of insurrection following the January 6 Capitol riot — and was acquitted by the Senate both times.

Following his 2020 defeat to Joe Biden, Trump announced a 2024 campaign in November 2022 and secured the Republican nomination despite facing multiple criminal indictments. He was convicted in May 2024 by a New York jury on 34 felony counts of falsifying business records, becoming the first former U.S. president to be convicted of a felony. He was sentenced in January 2025 to an unconditional discharge.

In the 2024 election, Trump defeated Vice President Kamala Harris, winning 312 Electoral College votes to Harris's 226 and becoming the first president since Grover Cleveland to serve two non-consecutive terms. His second term has focused on immigration enforcement, tariff imposition, federal workforce reduction, and withdrawal from international agreements.

For Massachusetts residents, the President leads the executive branch and signs or vetoes all federal legislation. Contact the White House at 202-456-1111 or visit whitehouse.gov.

sources.label: Official White House Website
White House — President Trump Official Bio

vicePresident.name: JD Vance
vicePresident.title: Vice President of the United States
vicePresident.office: Eisenhower Executive Office Building, Washington, DC 20501
vicePresident.background: Vice President of the United States since January 2025. Former U.S. Senator from Ohio (2023–2025). Author of 'Hillbilly Elegy.' Yale Law School graduate.
vicePresident.backgroundFull:
JD Vance is the 50th Vice President of the United States, having been inaugurated on January 20, 2025, alongside President Donald Trump. He previously served as U.S. Senator from Ohio from January 2023 to January 2025, and before that was best known as the author of the 2016 memoir Hillbilly Elegy.

Vance was born James Donald Bowman on August 2, 1984, in Middletown, Ohio — a Rust Belt city he later documented in his memoir. Raised partly by his maternal grandparents (his "Mamaw and Papaw") amid family instability and his mother's struggles with opioid addiction, he graduated from Middletown High School and enlisted in the U.S. Marine Corps, serving in Iraq as a public affairs officer from 2003 to 2007. He then earned a B.A. summa cum laude in political science and philosophy from Ohio State University (2009) and a J.D. from Yale Law School (2013), where he met his wife Usha Chilukuri.

After Yale, Vance worked as a lawyer and venture capitalist in San Francisco and Cincinnati, backed by PayPal co-founder Peter Thiel. His 2016 memoir Hillbilly Elegy: A Memoir of a Family and Culture in Crisis became a New York Times bestseller and was adapted into a Netflix film in 2020, bringing him national attention as a voice on white working-class culture in Appalachia and the Midwest.

Vance ran for the Republican Senate nomination in Ohio in 2022, reversing his earlier criticism of Donald Trump to earn Trump's endorsement, and won the general election over Democratic incumbent Tim Ryan. In the Senate he served on the Banking Committee and was known as an ardent Trump loyalist and opponent of military aid to Ukraine. Trump selected him as his running mate in July 2024 at the Republican National Convention.

As Vice President, Vance presides over the U.S. Senate and casts tie-breaking votes. He has been an active advocate for the Trump administration's domestic and foreign policy agenda and is widely considered a leading contender for the Republican presidential nomination in 2028.

For Massachusetts residents, the Vice President's office can be contacted at 202-456-1414 or through whitehouse.gov.

sources.label: Official White House Website
White House — Vice President Vance Official Bio
